questions.text: Which punctuation marks will be used to separate both the clauses in the following sentence?The gang was rounded up after the raid the leader escaped.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This is incorrect because a full stop is used to end a complete sentence, not a clause.
The comma is the correct punctuation mark to separate the clauses in the sentence.
Colons are punctuation marks used to signal when what comes next is directly related to the previous sentence.
Semicolon is used between closely related independent clauses which are not joined by a coordinating conjunction.

questions.explanation: A semicolon is most commonly used to link (in a single sentence) two independent clauses that are closely related in thought. A semicolon is used as a clause separator between two independent clauses in a sentence when both of the clauses are of equal importance.

questions.text: Pick the correct option:These are old those are new.
questions.subject: English

options.text: These, are old, those are new.
options.explanation: This option introduces unnecessary punctuation makes that disrupts the sentence structure and does not effectively convey the meaning.

options.text: These are old; those are new.
options.explanation: This option provides the correct punctuation to effectively separate the two statements and indicate a clear distinction between the items being referred to.

options.text: These are old: those are new.
options.explanation: This option introduces unnecessary punctuation mark (:) makes that disrupts the sentence structure and does not effectively convey the meaning.

options.text: These are new- those are new.
options.explanation: This is incomplete and lacks the necessary punctuation to separate the two statements.

questions.explanation: A semicolon is most commonly used to link (in a single sentence) two independent clauses that are closely related in thought.

questions.text: How can this sentence be improved using punctuations?She hoped to find a new job. One that would let her earn money during the school year.

options.text: job. One that
job. The kind that
job, one that
job, so that it

questions.explanation: Since both the sentences are related to each other, a comma could be added. A comma marks a slight break between different parts of a sentence.

questions.text: Spot the error.If you lend him a book, he will lend it to someone else and never you will get it back.
questions.subject: English

options.text: If you lend him a book
options.explanation: The segment “if you lend him a book” is correct because it properly introduces the conditional clause.

options.text: He will lend it
options.explanation: The phrase “he will lend it” is correct because it is the main clause of the conditional sentence. It follows the proper structure: “If [condition], [result]”. Changing it would make the sentence ungrammatical.

options.text: To someone else
options.explanation: The phrase “to someone else” is correct because it clearly indicates the recipient of the book. It properly completes the verb “lend”, which requires an indirect object. Omitting or changing it would make the sentence unclear or grammatically incorrect.

options.text: And never you will get it back
options.explanation: D is correct because in a sentence adverbs are usually written after the verb. So the correct pattern would be: and you will never get it back.

questions.explanation: Adverbs are placed after auxiliary verbs which include “will”. Hence, the correct option is "option D".

options.explanation: The lack of quotation marks makes it seem like the lyrics are part of the main sentence, rather than a quotation.

options.text: However
options.explanation: "However" is an adverb, and it's used correctly in the sentence to indicate a contrast or surprise.

options.text: Very

options.text: Have

options.text: None

options.text: Headmaster, Mr. welldon, however, took a broadminded view of my Latin prose.
options.explanation: This sentence and is correct in its use of commas to set off the appositive "Mr. welldon" and to introduce the modifier "however."Correct Answer: D) Headmaster, Mr. welldon, however, took a broadminded view of my Latin prose.Option D correctly uses commas to set off "Mr. welldon" as additional information about "Headmaster" and places "however" correctly to modify the entire clause that follows.

questions.explanation: This sentence is identical to and is correct in its use of commas to set off the appositive "Mr. welldon" and to introduce the modifier "however."Correct Answer: D) Headmaster, Mr. welldon, however, took a broadminded view of my Latin prose.Option D correctly uses commas to set off "Mr. welldon" as additional information about "Headmaster" and places "however" correctly to modify the entire clause that follows.

options.explanation: This option is correct. It uses a comma correctly after the introductory phrase "Just to be sure." questions.subject: English

options.text: Jan asked; “What did Joe mean when he said, ‘I will see you later.’ ”
options.explanation: The semicolon (;) is incorrect and should be replaced with a comma (,) to separate the two independent clauses.

options.text: Jan asked, “What did Joe mean when he said, ‘I will see you later?’ ”
options.explanation: The semicolon (;) is incorrect and should be replaced with a comma (,) to separate the two independent clauses.

options.text: Jan asked, “What did Joe mean when he said, ‘I will see you later’?”
options.explanation: This sentence is a correct example of nested quotation marks. The inner quotation marks (‘I will see you later’) set off the words that Joe said, and the outer quotation marks (“What did Joe mean...”) set off the question that Jan asked. The question mark at the end is correctly placed outside the outer quotation marks, indicating that it is part of the sentence as a whole, rather than part of the inner quotation.

options.text: Jan asked, “What did Joe mean when he said, ‘I will see you later’ ”?
options.explanation: The question mark is incorrectly placed outside the outer quotation marks with an unnecessary space

questions.explanation: Jan asked, “What did Joe mean when he said, ‘I will see you later’?” - This sentence is a correct example of nested quotation marks. The inner quotation marks (‘I will see you later’) set off the words that Joe said, and the outer quotation marks (“What did Joe mean...”) set off the question that Jan asked. The question mark at the end is correctly placed outside the outer quotation marks, indicating that it is part of the sentence as a whole, rather than part of the inner quotation.
